_meta.purpose: What each state charges an S CORPORATION ITSELF, on top of what its shareholders pay personally. Without this, an 'is an S-corp election worth it' calculator overstates the saving — in some states it inverts the answer entirely.
_meta.why: The verified 2026 dataset carries entity-level detail for Delaware and Wyoming only. Everything else here is researched against state revenue departments, recorded with its source and a confidence level, and left null wherever it could not be confirmed. A null means 'we do not know', which pages must say — it never means zero.
rules: entityLevelIncomeTaxRate is what the STATE charges the CORPORATION on its net income. It is not the shareholder's personal rate and it is not an optional PTET election.
minimumAnnualTax is owed regardless of profit, including at a loss, unless notes say otherwise.
A state that does not recognise the federal S election taxes the company as a C corporation. For those, the election can COST money rather than save it, and the page has to lead with that.
Null is a correct answer. Never interpolate a rate.
_meta.modellingNote: The comparison these figures feed is sole proprietorship vs S-corp. Where a state's minimum tax attaches to the LLC rather than the S election — California's $800 is owed by an LLC either way — the cost is not caused by electing, and the page must say so or it overstates the downside for anyone already trading through an LLC.

CA.notes: The annual tax is the GREATER of 1.5% of net income or $800 — not the sum. Owed whether the corporation is active, inactive or running at a loss, but waived for a newly formed S corporation's first taxable year. Note that an LLC in California owes an $800 annual tax anyway, so for someone already trading through an LLC the minimum is not a cost of electing.
sources: ftb.ca.gov — S corporations business type: 'California taxes every S corporation that has California source income at 1.5%'; 'the annual tax for S corporations is the greater of 1.5% of net income or $800'
ftb.ca.gov — 2025/2026 Form 100S booklet and Publication 1060 (first-year minimum waiver)

TN.notes: Tennessee does not recognise the federal S election and taxes an S corporation as a C corporation: 6.5% excise tax on net earnings, plus a franchise tax of the greater of $0.25 per $100 of net worth or $100. A $50,000 standard deduction against net earnings applies from tax year 2024. Electing S status in Tennessee creates a state tax rather than avoiding one — this is the clearest case where the federal saving can be wiped out.
sources: tn.gov Department of Revenue — Franchise and Excise Tax Manual (June 2025)
Secondary, consistent: nolo.com and s-corptax.com state guides
